title: Hybrid Physics and NN-Based Runtime Assurance for Integrated Vehicle Health Management
description: This innovation integrates a hybrid 6DoF-EoM physics-based and neural network-based RTA monitor as part of an advanced IVHM system onboard high-performance computing avionics of aircraft, UAVs, and autonomous flight systems to enable real-time fault detection, anomaly prediction, and adaptive protection. The system conducts live cross-verification of sensor and actuator behavior against physics-consistent predictions and data-driven estimates. This enables detection of anomalies such as sensor d

title: Long-Distance Autonomous LiDAR-Based Terrain Relative Navigation in a Subterranean Proving Ground
description: We request 0.24FTE and $2K ODC to further develop high-rate autonomous surface mobility and navigation technologies within Cathedral Caverns State Park, in collaboration with Alabama State Parks. The team will demonstrate long-distance autonomous GPS-denied terrain relative navigation using hardware slated to fly as a tech demo to the lunar surface on an Astrolabs rover in November 2025, as well as deliver a high resolution, high accuracy digital twin of the environment. The Cathedral Caverns ca

title: Phase I Design Study of Collapsible Cryogenic Storage for In-situ Resource Utilization
description: We are asking for $50k for four personnel to work for three months to complete a Phase I design study on the adaptability of inflatable softgoods materials technology for collapsible cryogenic storage tanks. As the technical expertise for such a study is dispersed across multiple disciplines and organizations within Marshall Space Flight Center, formulating a cross-discipline team from the spacecraft propulsion and inflatable habitat organizations will uniquely build MSFC's expertise for In-situ

title: Extending the HYPERFIRE Method to Nuclear Thermal Propulsion
description: Subscale rocket engine development testing is expensive, making subscale validation of their ground test facilities even more costly. Yet, the need for a well-proven design is obvious when the facility in question is the first Nuclear Thermal Propulsion (NTP) test stand to be built in 60+ years. The Hydrocarbon Propellants Enabling Reproduction of Flows in Rocket Engines (HYPERFIRE) method was developed under prior CIF funding to make subscale testing quicker, cheaper, and easier. It employs hea

title: Physics-informed LLM Agent for Space Operations
description: NASA's real-time missions demand exceptional technical and cognitive performance from personnel under high stress, which can lead to errors. As missions extend to the Moon, Mars, and deep space, resilient, human-centered decision support systems become critical, especially for enhancing crew autonomy without Earth support. While Large Language Models (LLMs) have advanced commercial operations, their potential in the space domain for human decision support remains largely unexplored.  Objectives:

title: Text-to-Spaceship: Accelerating Mission Development with AI
description: This proposal aims to revolutionize NASA Goddard's mission development by infusing AI and computational design to accelerate development cycles and realize game-changing performance improvements. The Text-to-Spaceship framework will guide NASA's transition from using AI as an assistant to implementing fully autonomous AI-powered mission development as AI progresses toward Artificial General Intelligence within the next decade. The approach begins by using Large Language Models to translate natur

title: Closed-Loop PHA/Regolith Composite In-Situ Manufacturing
description: Develop a polymer matrix composite (PMC) material based on poly(hydroxyalkanoate)s (PHA)—resins that can be biologically synthesized and recycled in space using waste materials—and Lunar or Martian Regolith for additive manufacturing

title: AI Thermal Prediction Module for Electric Motors
description: This project proposes to develop a machine learning-driven model for electric aircraft propulsion, aiming to bridge a critical modeling and validation gap in electrified aviation. Instead of relying on conventional empirical or finite element models, it trains neural networks on experimental datasets to create scalable, architecture-agnostic thermal predictions across different electric motor designs. Researchers seek to integrate the AI thermal modeul into the physics-based real-time powertrain

title: BONSAI - Boron Nitride Quantum Strain Accelerometer Instrument
description: This project aims to develop a novel quantum-enhanced inertial sensor, specifically an accelerometer, that could significantly outperform traditional MEMS sensors. By leveraging the quantum spin properties of defects in hexagonal Boron Nitride (hBN), a two-dimensional material, the team plans to create a new type of sensor. The core idea is to combine MEMS technology with a quantum sensing readout, where the movement of a MEMS proof mass is optically measured through the strain resonance respons

title: Adaptive Controls for Active Structural Control
description: This project presents an ambitious and highly integrated approach to advancing adaptive flight control for flexible structures using a 'fly-fix-fly' framework. It combines additive manufacturing, aeroelastic modeling, and real-time adaptive controls, positioning itself at the forefront of modern flight experimentation. The research aims to take AFRL's wind tunnel test wings to flight, allowing robust evaluation of structurally variable aeroelastic configurations, adaptive controls, and serving a

title: Matrix Converter Controls and Hardware Development for AC-to-AC Distribution
description: Design and develop a high efficiency, high reliability, and high-power density matrix converter (MC) for integrating high-power 3-phase AC space, lunar, and Martian power grids

title: Lunar DustCam
description: The proposed Lunar DustCam is a very low C-SWAP imaging system for in situ monitoring of sunlight scattered by naturally mobilized dust (e.g., from meteoroid impacts, electrostatic lofting and levitation) as well as “collateral” dust ejected by surface operations (e.g., lander plumes, astronaut and rover traverses, and ISRU). Lunar dust is known to be an exploration hazard on the Moon due to its deleterious effects on imaging systems, thermal radiators, and moving parts, such that characterizing

title: Enabling Real Time Machine Learning Operations for Transient Science
description: To address NASA's lack of MLOps for transient science, this project will develop and demonstrate an MLOps framework with generative AI and explainable AI (XAI) to autonomously filter transient events for the Roman High Latitude Survey, with the ultimate goal of transferring this capability to other NASA missions.

title: Microbial Event Monitor for Long-Duration Spaceflight and Lunar/Martian Habitats
description: Develop a Microbial Event Monitor (MEM) leveraging the PI's previously developed Endospore Viability Assay (EVA) technology to enable automated, continuous monitoring of microbial bioburden in spacecraft cabin environments.
technologyArea: Air, Water, Microbial, and Acoustic Sensors

title: Hydrogen Gas Generator for on Mars Methane Production
description: Human Mars missions require fuel to return to Earth and bringing fuel is not sustainable for long-duration campaigns. A solution for on-Mars fuel production could be available in-situ from mining water and electrolysis but it requires a high level of complexity and multiple missions to accomplish. A different solution is needed for early and mid-term missions. A possible solution is to combine hydrogen and atmospheric CO2 producing methane through the Sabatier process. To demonstrate this proces
technologyArea: Resource Processing for Production of Mission Consumables

title: autoNGC High Performance Spaceflight Computing board
description: Our proposal introduces a next-generation processor card leveraging NASA's High Performance Spaceflight Computing (HPSC) technology, designed to revolutionize spacecraft computing capabilities for both AutoNGC systems and broader applications in small satellite C&DH and IC&DH. This innovation consolidates functionality that currently requires two separate processor cards in AutoNGC into a single, high-performance computing solution. The card features eight RISC-V cores with 512-bit vector units

title: Development of Safe and Low Temperature Operational Battery for Mars Surface Mission
description: Enable high-capability Li-ion battery (250Wh/kg) operation throughout Martian day/night

title: Hypersonic Vehicle Navigation With Quantum Sensor
description: This proposal explores a cutting-edge quantum sensing approach—leveraging technologies such as atom interferometry or NV center-based magnetometers—to enable GPS-independent inertial navigation in hypersonic environments. The innovation has significant implications for defense, autonomous systems, and NASA’s hypersonic programs.

title: De-Coupled 10Boron Enriched 10B4C/SiC Diaphragm Sensor Concept for Nuclear Reactors
description: Develop radiation/temperature hardened, miniaturized, and integrated pressure/temperature sensors which are compact, autonomous, and operate reliably without maintenance for several years for nuclear power systems.

title: GPU-Accelerated Hypersonic Propulsion Modeling Capability
description: Capability Need/Knowledge Gap:  Hypersonic propulsion is a key technology development for both aeronautic and launch vehicles. The key challenge is the analysis of supersonic combustion stability, with heavy computational cost. Objectives: Develop a GPU-enabled, high performance scale-resolving Large-eddy simulation (LES) framework for supersonic turbulent combustion, validated against practical scramjet configurations.

title: Lensless Computational Imaging for Stereo Cameras for Lunar Plume-Surface Studies
description: Understanding the interaction of rocket engine plumes with the unimproved lunar regolith is a critical aspect of safely and successfully landing spacecraft on the Moon.  The Stereo Cameras for Lunar Plume-Surface Studies (SCALPSS) payload recently imaged the lunar surface during the descent and landing phases of the Firefly Blue Ghost Mission 1 in March of 2025.  Primary constraints placed on the SCALPSS payload are limits on mass and data downlink bandwidth, where payload mass is impacted by th

title: SHERPA RPA (Robust Precomputed Autonomy): Addressing the Complexities of Real-Time Mission Operations
description: The fundamental approach was validated in Phase I by implementing and testing a partially observable Markov decision process (POMDP) model of the Enceladus Orbilander life detection instrument suite (published in a 2025 IEEE Space Robotics Workshop paper). Decision policies were computed using Successive Approximations of the Reachable Space under Optimal Policies (SARSOP) algorithm. In Phase II, we will develop methods for hierarchical reasoning and handling interactions between systems (also v

title: Compact Optical Method for Long-range, Inexpensive Network Communication ​
description: This project develops a small, low-cost optical comm system for direct Low Earth Orbit (LEO) and/or Lunar to Earth data downlinks.

title: Trash Compaction and Processing System
description: The Mars Campaign Office, Logistics Reduction project called the Trash Compaction and Processing System (TCPS) is a waste management technology. Currently, there are no trash management practices that are being implemented in the space environment other than manual compaction of waste into a plastic bag. The current practice does not recover critical resources such as water, does not prevent the growth of potentially harmful microbiological pathogens, and provides only limited volume reductio

title: 90K Cryocooler Electronics and Unit Demonstration Development
description: This project's objective is to design a Flight Prototype 90K 150W RTB Cryocooler Control Electronics Unit. This unit will be designed enable Thermo-Mechanical Unit performance and operate reliably for the life of an anticipated Mars mission. The control electronics unit will be designed to operate in defined environments, while meeting power and weight efficiency requirements.

title: Low Leakage Hydrogen Valves
description: This project's main objective is to develop a seat and poppet design that allows for self center of the poppet head while maintaining the necessary contact stresses for a reliable and tight shutoff. This objective is accomplished through two major phases: (1) an in-house government development of the seat and poppet design, including testing for at least TRL 4 achievement, and (2) a competitive procurement of a high-fidelity prototype valve from a commercial partner which will enable a future

title: Alternative Cryocooler Development
description: The goal of the Alternative Cryocooler development activity is to broaden industry base and develop 20K and 90K Cryocooler alternatives - both various cycle and vendor alternatives - that can be infused into lunar and Mars missions. The activity will develop technology alternatives to existing Reverse Turbo-Brayton cycle 20K and 90K cryocooler systems, assess alternate concepts / supplier for components, and provide investment to continue the development of 90K and 20K cryocooler systems to a

title: LOXSAT 1
description: Small-scale flight demonstration of a complete cryogenic oxygen fluid management system. Develop, launch, and fly a 9 month technology demonstration as the primary payload on a Rocket Lab Photon satellite to collect critical CFM data in orbit while testing 10 different CFM technologies necessary for creating practical propellent depots.

title: Space Storm Solar Sail Sentinel (S5)
description: The Space Storm Solar Sail Sentinel (S5) will demonstrate the use of solar sails as a means of propulsion, specifically how a solar sail can be controlled and maneuvered for navigation.  The performance of a magnetometer in the space environment aboard a solar sail spacecraft will also be verified.  S5 will be a secondary payload on the SOLAR-A mission (Dec 2029) on a trajectory to non-Keplerian sub-L1 halo orbit.

title: Nuclear Thermal Propulsion Technology Maturation
description: NTP is an open-ended project focused on developing enabling technologies for nuclear thermal propulsion and demonstrating the robust functionality of those technologies through ground and flight testing. NTP systems have capabilities that can be directly leveraged, or readily evolved, for future NASA missions that include cis-lunar operating systems, deep-space science systems, and small and large cargo transportation systems for Mars human exploration. The technology challenges NTP is focuse
partners: Army Space and Missile Defense Command

title: Nuclear Electric Propulsion Technology Maturation
description: NEP is an open-ended project focused on developing critical and enabling technologies for nuclear electric propulsion leading to an integrated NEP vehicle system. NEP systems have capabilities that can be directly leveraged, or readily evolved, for future NASA missions that include cis-lunar operating systems and deep-space human and science systems. Electric thruster technology offers much higher propellant mass efficiencies when compared chemical and nuclear thermal options. Combining a nuc

title: ULA Vulcan Engine Reuse Scale (VERS) HIAD Technology Demonstration
description: The company will continue to evolve a proven Hypersonic Inflatable Aerodynamic Decelerator (HIAD) technology design. ULA will develop a larger 10-meter HIAD that leverages a two-piece structure to enable effective load distribution for even larger inflatable decelerators.

title: Electrically Actuated Quick-Disconnect Coupling for Cislunar and Lunar Surface Cryogenic Propellant Transfer
description:
The Electrically Actuated Quick-Disconnect Coupling is a very low leakage (Dry) make/break cryogenic fluid compatible coupling that has broad application for in-space and Lunar surface refueling. This technology enables the development of vehicle on-orbit and/or on-surface cryogenic fuel transfer/refueling capability.

Project includes multiple additively manufactured components, including two independent (male and female) dual-sealing housings designed for cryogenic propellant compatibi

title: Propellant Production Plant Tipping Point (PRO-TP)
description: Under the 2019 Blue Origin Tipping Point contract, Blue Origin's original goal was to build and test a full-scale In-Situ Resource Utilization (ISRU) liquid oxygen/liquid hydrogen (LO2/LH2) liquefaction and storage prototype testbed that could be used or replicated on the first flight Blue Moon descent lander. The test bed would use full-scale Blue Moon hardware and commercial cryocooler systems. This Tipping Point resulted in a contract descope. The goals of this activity after contract desc

title: On-Orbit Large-Scale Cryogenic Propellant Management and Transfer Demonstration
description: Demonstration of large-scale on-orbit cryogenic fluid transfer (more than 3 metric tons of liquid oxygen between tanks on a Starship in orbit) and cryogenic fluid management to provide a basis for operational use of in-space refueling technology, a key enabler for reusable, sustainable space transportation and the broader commercialization of space.

title: On-Orbit Servicing, Assembly, and Manufacturing Demonstration-1
description: The Restore Mission will demonstrate refueling and a relocation of the Landsat 7 satellite. Restore-L will launch to a nominal (LEO) altitude of 680 km and conduct its on-orbit checkout. Landsat 7 will descend to a servicing altitude of 685 km and enter a stable, solar-inertial attitude. Restore-L will then use both ground-generated and on-board navigation solutions to rendezvous with Landsat 7 and perform an autonomous capture. After client capture, the assigned refueling tasks will be perfo
technologyArea: Rendezvous, Proximity Operations, and Capture Flight and Ground Systems

title: Fission Surface Power
description: Fission Surface Power (FSP) project will advance the design, system integration, and test qualification for a nuclear powered electric generator capability that can be demonstrated on the surface of the Moon and that is extensible to NASA exploration needs for Mars. The FSP project will design, build and qualify an engineering flight unit (EFU) for a minimum 10-kWEpower production to be demonstrated on a lunar mission.
technologyArea: Other Advanced Concepts for Generating and Converting Power

title: Solar Electric Propulsion
description: The Solar Electric Propulsion Project shall develop and qualify an advanced 12.5 kW EP thruster applicable to human/robotic exploration and commercial spaceflight missions including the Power and Propulsion Element (PPE). The technology development objective of the SEP subproject, Plasma Diagnostics Package (PDP), is to develop, qualify, and deliver a flight PDP provided as GFE to PPE that can collect on-orbit PPE Ion Propulsion System (IPS) plasma data in the space environment.

title: High charge (Z) and Energy TRaNsport Physics Development
description: The High charge (Z) and Energy TRaNsport (HZETRN) Physics Development project creates and verifies improved nuclear physics models for NASA’s HZETRN code to support accurate assessment of crew radiation exposure in heavily shielded spacecraft. An accurate and computationally efficient radiation transport code is needed to support the design of spacecraft that keep radiation exposure As Low As Reasonably Achievable (ALARA) and to verify spacecraft radiation protection requirements. NASA’s HZET
technologyArea: Human Health, Life Support, and Habitation Systems

title: Advanced Trace Contaminant Control
description: Trace Contaminant Control (TCC) is typically activated carbon and some level of catalyst to adsorb and react contaminants in the air phase. The current state-of-the-art carbon dioxide (CO2) removal sorbents utilize amines that rely on a hard vacuum to desorb during an Extravehicular Activity (EVA). Some sorbents also act to remove some trace contaminants. This may be severely limited on Mars due to the atmosphere. Therefore, Mars will require new methods, sorbents, and testing of the

title: Crew State & Risk Model
description: Physiological inputs and outputs are adequately known for International Space Station (ISS) mission scenarios and tasks, however, planetary exploration missions will be significantly different in terms of systems, tasks, and environments. The communication latency between Earth and Mars will necessitate a level of Extravehicular Activity (EVA) autonomy well beyond our current capability. The Crew State and Risk Model (CSRM) is a collection of core physiologic models and will provide individua
technologyArea: Human Health, Life Support, and Habitation Systems

title: Lunar OutpOst Neutron Spectrometer
description: The objective of the Lunar OutpOst Neutron Spectrometer (LOONS) task is to fully develop, test, and calibrate a fast neutron spectrometer for characterizing the albedo neutron environment on the lunar surface. LOONS uniquely addresses both scientific and operational needs including a broad range of scientific applications from solar to planetary in addition to a critical need for radiation monitoring in deep space. LOONS is an imaging neutron spectrometer based on the double-scatter technique

title: Integrated Solar Energetic Particle Warning System
description: In 2018 under the Mars Campaign Office RadWorks activity, the Johnson Space Center (JSC) / Space Radiation Analysis Group (SRAG) and Goddard Space Flight Center (GSFC) / Community Coordinated Modeling Center (CCMC) groups re-established the Integrated Solar Energetic Particle (Warning System) (ISEP) collaboration to expand SRAG’s current space weather monitoring capabilities for Beyond-Low-Earth Orbit (LEO) missions. The project initially focused on transitioning both operational and research

title: Crew Health And Performance Exploration Analog
description: Missions for Moon to Mars are very different from missions to the ISS due to length of isolation and confinement, distance from Earth, and the resource restrictions that will be applied to the crew. Artemis Program vehicles have limited mass allocations for food and other consumables. Given the increased distance to Mars and timeline for return, programs need to understand the impacts of restrictions within realistic constraints on health and performance to inform risk/resource trades prior t

title: Mars Food Refrigeration
description: Food and nutrition are critical to health and performance and therefore the success of human space exploration. Some quality factors and nutrients within the shelf-stable, packaged food system currently in use on the International Space Station (ISS) can degrade over long timelines when stored at 21°C, which can impact nutritional intake and ultimately health and performance. Food degradation is a complex chemical kinetics and thermodynamic process which can be slowed by reducing temperature.

title: Ohalo III, The First Operational Crop Production System and Prototype for a Mars Transit Vehicle
description: Food and nutrition are critical to health and performance and therefore the success of human space exploration. However, the shelf-stable food system currently in use on the International Space Station (ISS) is not sustainable as missions become longer and further from Earth, even with modification for mass and water efficiencies. Bioregenerative foods as part of the astronaut diet are expected to provide whole food nutrition, improve menu variety, and positively impact behavioral health. Sig
technologyArea: Human Health, Life Support, and Habitation Systems

title: Forced Air Breathing Mask
description:
NASA has developed a specialized set of emergency response equipment to address fire‑related hazards in spacecraft. Because human spaceflight presents unique operational and safety challenges, much of this equipment has been designed and maintained in‑house as Government Furnished Equipment (GFE).

The overall objective is to leverage the 20 plus years of breathing mask experience in the Crew and Thermal Systems Division to identify a new exploration atmosphere ready forced air breathing

title: Spacecraft Materials Textiles
description: The Spacecraft Textile Materials Development for Exploration project focuses on non-flammable IVA textile development and optimization for use in the Exploration Atmosphere (up to 37% O2/8.2 psia), intended for infusion and use in Artemis missions and beyond. A sampling of the textiles needed to outfit a spacecraft cabin include knit and woven fabrics, sewing threads, nonwoven felts/foams including acoustic absorption material, seam sealing tape/fabric, webbing, bungee/elastic cord, and hook

title: Cold Trap Smoke Eater
description:
A spacecraft fire is particularly hazardous because in addition to the hazardous gaseous and aerosol combustion products that are introduced into the cabin environment, a fire can damage not only the equipment that is the source of the fire but also the systems adjacent to it and poses a serious risk to crew health and safety. Having effective post-fire clean-up equipment and techniques is necessary to remove harmful contaminants and return to a safe cabin atmosphere.


A cold trap

title: Partial Gravity Portable Fire Suppression System
description: All NASA space vehicles have had and will have either handheld portable and/or fixed fire suppression systems. But while each vehicle is required to have a suppression system there are no standardized designs or performance standards for spacecraft suppression systems. For example the design of the International Space Station (ISS) Carbon Dioxide (CO2) fire extinguisher is based on meeting the National Fire Protection Agency standard for a CO2 extinguisher discharged behind an ISS standard pa

title: Fire Standard Database
description: The Fire Standard Database project will develop a web-based application that provides datasets, analysis tools, and example models to support spacecraft fire safety design and certification. The NASA Engineering and Safety Center (NESC) is currently developing a spacecraft fire safety certification standard for future spacecraft and habitats that will include both prescriptive and performance-based requirements. These requirements will require vehicle programs to demonstrate through analysis

title: Fire Detection System
description: Early detection of a developing fire is crucial for crew safety. Current spacecraft smoke detectors are hindered by the broad variety of combustion particles emitted from a spacecraft fire, the unique transport dynamics of reduced gravity, the specific vehicle or habitat geometries, and nuisance false alarms caused by dust and other non-combustion particles. This project will develop a “smart” spacecraft smoke detection system with a lower nuisance alarm rate than the current state of the art

title: Post Fire Cleanup Kit
description: A spacecraft fire is particularly hazardous due to the inherent danger of fires in closed environments. Having effective post-fire clean-up equipment and techniques is critical as acid gases are very corrosive and stick to surfaces. This could damage hardware or electronics or pose a long-term risk to crew health on exploration duration missions. Post-fire clean-up requires a two-stage approach for clean-up of both the vehicle atmosphere and also clean-up and decontamination of surfaces and p
technologyArea: Human Health, Life Support, and Habitation Systems

title: Emergency Breathing Apparatus
description: NASA has developed a set of emergency response equipment to address spacecraft fire safety hazards. Because of the unique requirements of human spaceflight, much of this equipment has been developed in-house by NASA and maintained as Government Furnished Equipment (GFE). Human spaceflight missions beyond Low Earth Orbit (LEO) have spacecraft fire safety requirements that are more severe and more challenging. Without an opportunity to quickly return to earth, spacecraft fire safety systems mus

title: Partial-g Fire Detection
description: Early detection of a developing fire is critical for ensuring that a fire does not result in crew death or injury. A comprehensive lunar fire detection strategy involves selection of the most effective detection technology (e.g., ionization, photometric, or a combination of multiple sensors), detector placement (e.g., behind returns or on ceilings), and appropriate alarm thresholds to optimize early fire detection. In addition, false alarms due to lofted cabin dust challenge reliable fire det

title: Acid Gas Detection & Absorption
description:
A spacecraft fire is particularly hazardous because in addition to the hazardous gaseous and aerosol combustion products that are introduced into the cabin environment, a fire can damage not only the equipment that is the source of the fire but also the systems adjacent to it and poses a serious risk to crew health and safety. 

The fate of acid gases in a spacecraft following a fire is not completely understood.  Terrestrial fire research is primarily concerned about the immediate effec

title: Fire Scenario Modeling
description: Future missions to the Moon and Mars pose enhanced fire safety risks due to longer durations and increased oxygen. Full scale fire testing, such as those performed during previous space programs, is the most straightforward way to obtain an understanding of this risk. These tests are difficult to implement in 1-g and even more challenging to attempt in reduced gravity. The ability to model fire scenarios using a vehicle mock-up would enable commercial partners to design more effective fire sa

title: Crew Connectedness Adv Comm Platform
description: Exploration missions will feature austere habitable environments, small volumes, mass constraints, limitations in infrastructure (such as bandwidth limitations) and communications delay that will impose challenges to evaluating and maintaining crew's behavioral health and performance.  Future space exploration, including missions to Mars, will include a significant communication delay between astronauts and those on Earth (e.g., friends and family, medical and psychological support providers,
technologyArea: Human Health, Life Support, and Habitation Systems
partners: U.S. Air Force 59th Medical Wing of 711th Human Performance Wing

title: Crew Health and Performance Integrated Data System Platform
description: Future exploration missions to Mars will have increased need for crew autonomy. Crew Health & Performance (CHP) related data on the ISS is currently downlinked manually and is in disparate locations, which limits crew autonomy for future missions. The CHP-IDSP project is developing a backend data system platform that grants the ability to seamlessly collect, store, process, and display CHP-related data for exploration missions. This platform allows for integration of data and advanced analyt
technologyArea: Human Health, Life Support, and Habitation Systems

title: Ohalo III - Sierra Space Hydroponic/Aeroponic Nutrient Delivery in Volumetrically Efficient Garden Root Module Phase B Grant
description: Food and nutrition are critical to health and performance and therefore the success of human space exploration. However, the shelf-stable food system currently in use on the International Space Station (ISS) is not sustainable as missions become longer and further from Earth, even with modification for mass and water efficiencies. Bioregenerative foods as part of the astronaut diet are expected to provide whole food nutrition, improve menu variety, and positively impact behavioral health. Sig
technologyArea: Human Health, Life Support, and Habitation Systems

title: CUBES-II: Center for the Utilization of Biological Engineering in Space v. II
description:
The Center for the Utilization of Biological Engineering in Space (CUBES) will leverage partnerships between NASA, other federal agencies, industry, and academia to:
1. Support biomanufacturing for deep space exploration;
2. Advance the practicality of an integrated, multi-function, multi-organism biomanufacturing system on a Mars mission; and
3. Showcase a continuous and semiautonomous biomanufacturing of fuel, materials, pharmaceuticals, and food in Mars-like conditions.
technologyArea: In Situ Manufacturing, Maintenance, and Repair

title: Formaldehyde Monitor
description: Formaldehyde (HCHO) is a mucosal irritant generated by off-gassing and metabolic processes and is closely tracked during human spaceflight, as the acceptable limit for crew exposure is low. HCHO monitoring is a requirement for all Moon-to-Mars programs, particularly as the potential for dormancy and lowered pressures in Exploration vehicles could lead to increased off-gassing of HCHO. However, the legacy NASA methods for HCHO detection in spacecraft, which depend on sample return for analysis
technologyArea: Air, Water, Microbial, and Acoustic Sensors

title: Joining Demonstrations In-Space
description: The company will demonstrate in-space component joining and inspection technologies for structural, electrical, and fluid systems. The capability would reduce risk and advance the maturity and reliability of in-space assembly architectures.

title: Infrastructure Manufacturing with Lunar Regolith - Mason
description: The company will develop a grader, compactor, and microwave emitter into a scalable platform that removes rocks, compacts loose regolith, and melts or sinters regolith into a solid surface. This technology could enable dust mitigation areas, habitat foundations, roads, and landing pads.

title: Spacecraft Water Impurities Monitor
description: The Mars Campaign Office (MCO) Life Support Systems project Environmental Monitoring (EM) systems task objectives are to develop and demonstrate onboard monitoring, detection, and analysis capabilities that will replace the state-of-the-art (SOA) need to frequently return water samples to Earth for ground-based laboratory analysis. This effort is addressing these challenges by adopting new analytical technologies and techniques that will allow for a modular EM system architecture that integra
technologyArea: Air, Water, Microbial, and Acoustic Sensors

title: Exploration Aerosol Monitors
description: The Mars Campaign Office (MCO) Life Support Systems project Environmental Monitoring (EM) systems task objectives are to develop and demonstrate onboard monitoring, detection, and analysis capabilities that will replace the state-of-the-art (SOA) need to frequently return air samples to Earth for ground-based laboratory analysis. This effort is addressing these challenges by adopting new analytical technologies and techniques that will allow for a modular EM system architecture that integrate
technologyArea: Air, Water, Microbial, and Acoustic Sensors

title: Four Bed CO2 Scrubber
description:
The Four Bed Carbon Dioxide (ops nom FBCO2) Scrubber has been developed as a technology demonstration flight system for the purpose of evaluating its potential as a carbon dioxide removal technology for the International Space Station (ISS) and future missions to the Moon and Mars. It was launched in August 2021 and activated in September of 2021.

The operation of a FBCO2 can be explained with the aid of the schematic shown in Figure 1. The FBCO2 continuously removes carbon dioxide (CO2
technologyArea: Human Health, Life Support, and Habitation Systems

title: PSI Instrumentation
description: The Plume-Surface Interactions Instrumentation (PSI-INST) project is focused on the development of PSI sensor technology for both near and far term applications, including both lunar (e.g., CLPS, HLS, Artemis) and Mars (e.g., Mars Sample Return). The project focus/goals include the following: 1)     Collection of PSI measurements during ground test opportunities.2)     Development of improved sensors and measurement capabilities for incorporation into payloads to capture additional flight data o
technologyArea: Instrumentation and Health Monitoring for EDL

title: Complex Curvature Retractable Pin Tool Friction Stir Welding Pathfinder Campaign
description: The focus of the project is to develop advanced Friction Stir Welding (FSW) Techniques for welding light weight, difficult to weld aluminum alloys in a complex three-dimensional geometry. The primary application for this technology is to weld geometries that are capable of radially accommodating essential human spaceflight capabilities (e.g., docking ports, hatches, windows, etc.). With the completion of this project Boeing plans to implement this capability into their habitat product line. Init

title: Motors for Dusty & Extremely Cold Environments
description: Develop an unheated magnetically-geared motor and an unheated piezoelectric motor that can operate continuously for a long duration at an ambient temperature of -240 ºC (33 K).Magnetically-geared and piezoelectric motors will utilize an inherently lubricant free gearbox at lower temperature for longer times (not including bearings). The rotational actuators will be evaluated in a controlled, reproducible, representative lunar dust environment. Conducting science in permanently shadowed and polar

title: Stereo CAmera for Lunar Plume-Surface Studies
description: Stereo imaging of Plume Surface Interactions (PSI) during and through CLPS lunar descent and landing. High frame-rate imaging is to begin at an altitude above where PSI onset is expected in order to capture the morphology of the disturbed terrain. This imaging continues through lander descent in order to capture PSI onset, measure morphology changes, and determine the extent of surface obscuration. High frame-rate imagines continues through landing and dust settling in order to capture the morph
technologyArea: Instrumentation and Health Monitoring for EDL

title: Dragonfly Entry Aerosciences Measurements
description: The New Frontiers Dragonfly mission to Titan, funded by the Science Mission Directorate (SMD) and led by the Applied Physics Laboratory (APL), includes an Engineering Science Investigation (ESI) called DrEAM, or Dragonfly Entry Aerosciences Measurements. DrEAM will provide key aerodynamic and aerothermodynamic data and assess the thermal protection system (TPS) performance on both the Dragonfly forebody and backshell. NASA's Ames Research Center (ARC) leads the DrEAM development effort and are p
technologyArea: Instrumentation and Health Monitoring for EDL
partners: Johns Hopkins University: Applied Physics Laboratory

title: Bifurcated Reversible Alkaline Cell for Energy Storage
description: pH Matter will design and demonstrate the reversible fuel cell technology in a full-scale 2-kW electrolysis (1-kW fuel cell) breadboard prototype system, increasing the technology from TRL 3 to TRL 4 or greater for lunar surface applications.  Reversible fuel cells (RFC) can store energy at 5 times higher energy density, but traditional systems require multiple components that are prone to failure. Additionally, in situ resource utilization (ISRU) will be needed to generate propellants and oxyge

title: Cooperative Autonomous Distributed Robotic Exploration
description: Multi-agent autonomy will be a key tool for future landed planetary exploration missions to maximize resource utilization to better enable systems and instruments operating within extreme mass, volume, time, and power constraints, while minimizing programmatic and technical risk. This includes autonomous robotic operations of navigation, communication, computation, perception, and decision-making. This increased capability enables individual robot autonomy and multi agent autonomy that allows th

title: Moon-to-Mars Planetary Autonomous Construction Technology
description: The Moon-to-Mars Planetary Autonomous Construction Technology (MMPACT) project is a NASA Space Technology Mission Directorate (STMD) Game Changing Development (GCD) project led by MSFC with partners including ICON Technology of Austin TX, Dr. Holly Shulman of Blue Star Advanced Manufacturing (BSAM), and other companies and universities in lesser roles. MMPACT is managed at Marshall Space Flight Center (MSFC) through the Science and Technology Office (STO). The MMPACT project focuses on the utili
partners: Louisiana State University and Agricultural & Mechanical College

title: Tethered Power Systems for Lunar Mobility and Power Transmission
description: The Tethered Power Systems for Lunar Mobility and Power Transmission (TYMPO) Project will develop a novel tether-based power transmission system to provide power over several meters to several kilometers to serve remote loads. The designed end-to-end tether power system will efficiently (> 90 %) deliver power (100 W – 10 kW) and communications across long (< 10 km) tethers to enable high-power transmission capabilities for fission or solar surface generation systems and provide rover access to e

title: Integrated Logistics Technology Analysis Task
description: STMD is supporting the Strategy and Architecture Office's (SAO) Integrated Logistics Pre-Project Team (ILPPT) in maturing concepts for conditioning and transporting consumables on the lunar surface from cargo landers to pressurized volumes. Logistics items include consumables, outfitting, maintenance and spare equipment, utilization (science, research, support items, etc), packaging, overhead and pressurized carriers for transportation, and storage. technologyArea: Human Health, Life Support, and Habitation Systems

title: Biomechanics, Ergonomics, and Anthropometrics for Mars Suits
description: Mars surface missions will require crew to perform frequent, physically and cognitively demanding a ExtraVehicular Activities (EVAs) in a gravity environment more than twice that of the Moon, following extended exposure to microgravity during transit. Current suit and hardware capabilities are not yet aligned with the expected operational demands and physiological challenges of Martian surface Crew Health and Performance/Spacesuits exploration. technologyArea: Human Health, Life Support, and Habitation Systems

title: Mars 2020 Ground Test Unit
description: On the Mars Perseverance rover, a suite of five spacesuit materials is being exposed to the environment and spectroscopy data is being taken by the SHERLOC (Scanning Habitable Environments with Raman & Luminescence for Organics & Chemicals) instrument. This project will: (1) Secure a corresponding ground test experiment repeating this exposure in a simulated Mars chamber; (2) Evaluate exposed materials for changes; and (3) Correlate in-situ vs. experimental spectroscopy SHERLOC data.
